preFix.title: Deep litter composting in the chicken run
preFix.subtitle: Letting bedding compost in place under the flock — a permanent in-run composting system
preFix.excerpt: Deep litter is a composting method where the chicken run floor is built up with successive layers of carbon-rich bedding material, wood chips, straw, dried leaves, and the chickens' manure and scratching activity keep the pile active. Done well, the heap heats to 40 to 50°C inside: the chickens eat the emerging insect larvae, and finished compost is harvested from the base of the run once or twice a year. It reduces smell, provides a dry scratching surface, and eliminates the need to muck out a separate heap.
preFix.sourceNotes: Harvey Ussery, The Small-Scale Poultry Flock (2011), chapter on deep litter. Joel Salatin, Pastured Poultry Profit$ (1993), principles of in-situ composting. Organic Research Centre guidance on small flock management.

content.text: Chickens scratch and turn organic matter as a natural behaviour.

turns that into a composting system. Add carbon material (wood chips, straw, autumn leaves, torn cardboard) on top of accumulated manure. The chickens mix it as they scratch. Microbial activity at the base produces heat and breaks down the material. The result is a dry, odour-free floor and a supply of partially composted material to harvest each year.
Setting up the deep litter bed

The run floor should be earthen or fine gravel, not concrete. The microbes and worms in the soil beneath the deep litter are part of the system. If the run is currently on concrete, add 100 mm of soil as a base layer before beginning.
Start with a 100 to 150 mm base layer of wood chips (the best carbon source, large chips resist compaction and last months). Straw works but packs more quickly into an anaerobic mat.
Add a thin scattering of kitchen grain scraps or scratch feed on top to encourage the flock to work the surface immediately.

The rule is: when the surface is wet, slimy, or smells of ammonia, add more carbon. A 50 to 100 mm fresh layer of wood chips buries the wet material and the chickens mix it in within a day. You should never smell a well-maintained deep litter system from more than 1 m away.

in a deep litter run with 4 to 6 hens and weekly wood chip additions runs itself without calculation, the chickens produce manure at a consistent rate and the wood chips you add by eye balance it. If the run smells, add carbon. If the material is bone-dry and not breaking down at the base, wet it or add some kitchen waste for a nitrogen boost.

Once or twice a year (typically spring and autumn), remove the lower layer of the deep litter bed using a garden fork. The base material, dark, crumbly, sweet-smelling, is partially finished compost. Move it to a separate heap for 4 to 8 weeks of curing before applying to the garden. Apply the standard maturity checks (smell, germination test) before using on edible crops.

Deep litter does not increase red mite (Dermanyssus gallinae) risk, red mite lives in cracks in the coop woodwork, not in the run bedding. However, a deep litter bed at the base of the coop steps can harbour litter beetles and ground mites. Keep the coop floor clean and treat the coop annually with an appropriate red mite product regardless of the run management method.
